---
title: "Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"
subtitle: "Overview"
---

# Objective

This chapter introduces the discipline behind a usable backtest:

1. define a trading hypothesis before inspecting results
2. implement the signal with only available historical information
3. match the backtest assumptions to the execution venue and order type
4. review costs, constraints, and statistical reliability before trusting the result

# Sections

## 1.1 Backtesting as a Research Loop

[Open web notes](sections/01-backtesting-as-research-loop/web-notes.qmd)

The first section explains why a backtest must specify implementation details,
avoid look-ahead and data-snooping bias, and stay close to executable trading
logic.

Outputs:
[video](sections/01-backtesting-as-research-loop/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[web](sections/01-backtesting-as-research-loop/web-notes.qmd),
[PDF](sections/01-backtesting-as-research-loop/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 1.2 Net Returns and Costs

[Open web notes](sections/02-net-returns-costs/web-notes.qmd)

The second section separates gross signal behavior from transaction costs,
slippage, and position timing.

Outputs:
[video](sections/02-net-returns-costs/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[web](sections/02-net-returns-costs/web-notes.qmd),
[PDF](sections/02-net-returns-costs/pdf-notes.qmd)

# Output Links

Each section owns its own video slides, web notes, and PDF notes. Use the
section folders as the source of truth; shared chapter pages are only indexes.
